did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used convolutional neural networks (CNNs) to discriminate basal cell carcinoma, Bowen's disease, and benign skin lesions from hyperspectral imaging data. Specifically, it employed 2D and 3D CNN branches with leave-one-out cross-validation to classify pixel-wise and majority-voting interpretations of skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The article is explicitly labeled as a 'RESEARCH LETTER' and presents original experimental results from a clinical study involving 130 lesions from 73 patients. It details the use of a 3D hyperspectral imaging system and convolutional neural networks to discriminate skin lesions, reporting specific diagnostic accuracy metrics (sensitivity, specificity, PPV) and statistical comparisons (p-values) derived from the authors' own data collection and analysis.

title: Discriminating basal cell carcinoma and Bowen's disease from benign skin lesions with a 3D hyperspectral imaging system and convolutional neural networks
brief_summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic performance of a 3D hyperspectral imaging system combined with convolutional neural networks (HSI-CNN) to discriminate nodular basal cell carcinoma, Bowen's disease, and benign skin lesions. The dataset comprised 130 lesions from 73 patients at Helsinki University Hospital, including nodular basal cell carcinomas, intradermal nevi, Bowen's disease, and seborrheic keratoses. The AI model utilized 2D and 3D CNN branches with leave-one-out cross-validation to perform pixel-wise and majority-voting classifications. In the majority-voting interpretation, the system achieved 85% sensitivity and 92% specificity for discriminating nodular basal cell carcinoma and intradermal nevi from healthy skin, and 84% sensitivity and 94% specificity for Bowen's disease and seborrheic keratoses. The results demonstrate that 3D-HSI-CNN shows potential for automated skin cancer diagnosis, particularly in primary health care settings.

lesion_types_studied.details: 32 lesions included in the study; used in experimental setup with intradermal nevi and healthy skin.
28 lesions included in the study; used in experimental setup with nodular basal cell carcinoma and healthy skin.
30 lesions included in the study; used in experimental setup with seborrheic keratoses and healthy skin.
40 lesions included in the study; used in experimental setup with Bowen's disease and healthy skin.
Used as the negative control class in both experimental setups (nBCC/ID vs healthy; BD/SK vs healthy).

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions (nodular basal cell carcinoma, intradermal nevus, Bowen's disease, seborrheic keratosis, and healthy skin).
all_task_types.details: The study employs convolutional neural networks (CNNs) to perform pixel-wise and majority-voting classification of hyperspectral imaging data. The model predicts discrete classes for lesions (nBCC, ID, BD, SK) versus healthy skin. Performance is evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value (PPV), and accuracy, with confusion matrices provided for the classification results.

datasets_used.name: Helsinki University Hospital Cohort
datasets_used.description: A private clinical dataset comprising 3D hyperspectral imaging (HSI) data cubes and corresponding histologically proven diagnoses collected from adult patients at the Department of Dermatology, Helsinki University Hospital. The data includes spatial resolution of 24 × 24 µm/px and 33 spectral wavebands plus depth information.

use.training: Used for training convolutional neural networks (CNNs) via leave-one-out cross-validation (LOO) on pixel-wise sub-cubes extracted from the lesions.
use.validation: Used for validation via leave-one-out cross-validation (LOO) where each lesion served as the test set in turn while the rest were used for training.
use.testing: Used for testing via leave-one-out cross-validation (LOO) and majority-voting interpretations of the pixel-wise results.
use.other: Used for sensitivity analysis by excluding 25 lesions with prior biopsies (13 nBCC and 12 BD) to compare outcomes against the primary analysis.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used for training and validating the convolutional neural networks (CNNs) by iteratively leaving out one lesion as the test set while training on the remaining lesions.
Calculated for sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value (PPV), and accuracy metrics, assuming the measurements follow a normal distribution.
Used to calculate p-values comparing the diagnostic performance of the RGB-CNN analysis to the HSI-CNN analysis.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in diagnostic performance between the HSI-CNN analysis and the RGB-CNN analysis.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a difference in diagnostic performance between the HSI-CNN analysis and the RGB-CNN analysis.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The HSI-CNN results were statistically significantly superior to those of RGB-CNN in pixel-wise analyses (p < 0.01), but comparable in majority voting analysis (p = 0.15). No significant difference was observed in the sensitivity analysis excluding lesions with prior biopsies (p = 0.16/0.27).

Helsinki University Hospital Cohort (nBC.notes: In the main majority voting results, the system reached 85% sensitivity and 92% specificity. The HSI-CNN results were statistically significantly superior to RGB-CNN in pixel-wise analyses (p < 0.01) but comparable in majority voting analysis (p = 0.15).

Helsinki University Hospital Cohort (BD,.notes: In the main majority voting results, the system reached 84% sensitivity and 94% specificity. The HSI-CNN results were statistically significantly superior to RGB-CNN in pixel-wise analyses (p < 0.01) but comparable in majority voting analysis (p = 0.15).

architecture_used.main_model: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with 2D and 3D branches (HSI-CNN and RGB-CNN variants)
architecture_used.input_size: 30 × 30 × 34 HS-sub-cubes (spatial resolution 24 × 24 µm/px, 33 spectral wavebands plus depth information)

architecture_details.input_type: Hyperspectral imaging (HSI) data cubes and RGB data cubes
architecture_details.data_processing: Random selection of 500 pixels from each lesion and 200 pixels from healthy skin; formation of 30x30x34 sub-cubes around the middle pixel including spectrum and depth information

classification_or_output_head.type: Pixel-wise classification and Majority voting interpretation
classification_or_output_head.details: Pixel-wise results obtained from the CNN; majority voting calculated by classifying every fifth pixel of each HS lesion cube and determining the class based on the majority pixels in the whole lesion

preprocessing_techniques: Exclusion of lesions with other histological diagnoses or imaging artefacts
Exclusion of 13/143 lesions due to low image quality
Annotation of HS lesion data cubes by a physician
Random selection of 500 pixels from each lesion and 200 pixels from healthy skin
Formation of 30 × 30 × 34 HS-sub-cubes around selected pixels including spectrum and depth information
Analysis of the middle pixel with its spectrum and depth information
Classification of every fifth pixel for majority voting interpretation
conclusions: The 3D hyperspectral imaging system combined with convolutional neural networks (3D-HSI-CNN) shows potential for automated skin cancer diagnosis, particularly in primary health care settings.

The system achieved 85% sensitivity and 92% specificity for discriminating nodular basal cell carcinoma and intradermal nevi from healthy skin, and 84% sensitivity and 94% specificity for Bowen's disease and seborrheic keratoses in majority-voting interpretations.
The HSI-CNN results were statistically significantly superior to RGB-CNN in pixel-wise analyses (p < 0.01) but comparable in majority voting analysis (p = 0.15).
The study demonstrates that the system can differentiate skin lesions of both epithelial and melanocyte cell origin.
The system achieved higher specificities than other optical methods such as reflectance confocal microscopy (RCM) and optical coherence tomography (OCT).
The 3D-HSI-CNN method does not require personal training or expertise from the user, serving as an easy-to-use automated analysis tool.

The integration of 3D shape data into spectral data allows the SICSURFIS imager to overcome previous challenges with imaging uneven surfaces, resulting in only 9% of lesions being excluded due to low image quality.
Broad studies with various lesion types and ethnicities, as well as head-to-head comparisons with other imaging modalities, are required to verify the system’s performance in clinical practice.
important_remarks: The study is a single-center cohort from Helsinki University Hospital, limiting generalizability; the authors explicitly state that broad studies with various ethnicities are required.
The dataset is small (130 lesions from 73 patients), which may limit the robustness of the deep learning model.
Leave-one-out cross-validation (LOO) was used, which can introduce optimistic bias in performance estimates for small datasets.
A sensitivity analysis excluding 25 lesions with prior biopsies showed improved performance for the RGB-CNN comparator, suggesting potential bias in the primary comparison.
The system achieved higher specificities than RCM and OCT in this study, but the authors note that direct comparison is difficult due to different datasets.
Research data are not shared, preventing independent verification of the results.
The study focuses on non-pigmented lesions (nBCC, ID, BD, SK), whereas most prior AI dermatology research has focused on pigmented lesions.
The paper is a 'Research Letter', which typically has shorter word counts and less detailed methodological reporting than full-length articles.
Confidence intervals were calculated assuming a normal distribution, which may not be appropriate for binomial proportions in small samples.
The model does not require user expertise, but its deployment in primary care requires validation in real-world, uneven-surface scenarios beyond the controlled study setting.
